next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 12

The word she was looking for was aluminum.

Not an important word. She'd been trying to describe the foil over the breakfast tray to her roommate Margaret, who couldn't hear her and wouldn't have cared, and the word had gone missing somewhere between her brain and her mouth. She'd stood there with her hand out, miming the act of peeling back the foil, and what came out was "the — the shiny. The paper that's not paper."

Margaret hadn't looked up from her oatmeal.

Lola sat on her bed after breakfast and tried the word again. Aluminum. There it was, back in place, as if it had only stepped out for a minute. Her heart jumped under her nightgown. She couldn't tell if the word had slipped because she'd been performing confusion for fourteen months and the act had worn a track through her, or because she was seventy-nine and had Parkinson's and sometimes seventy-nine-year-old women with Parkinson's lost words. Both belonged in the room. There was no test for sorting them. No blood draw, no brain scan, no doctor saying this one is the disease and this one, Dolores, is what you taught yourself to do.

She held her left hand up in front of her face. The tremor was there, as it always was — a fine, persistent shaking, worse in the mornings before her carbidopa-levodopa kicked in, worse when she was tired, worse when she was afraid. Right now it was moderate. A ripple, not a quake. She watched it and felt nothing useful. You couldn't argue with a tremor. You couldn't ask it to explain itself. It was just there. The medication helped and didn't help, and some mornings sh...
open_hooks_to_carry: Burt's fall at 3 AM — scalp laceration, Earl pressing the call bell
Carmen realizes the pill stash is hidden in Burt's room

payoffs_to_land.description: The Hungarian-English dictionary from Burt's wife, established as meaningful in Ch 3, is the pill stash hiding place Carmen must grab before EMTs arrive

prev_tail_excerpt:
...er hand back in her vest pocket.

"I'm not asking you for anything," Rosa said. "I get it. You've got your own situation. Your license, whatever. The old man said you were worried. Fine. Be worried. I'm telling you what I'm doing. I'm buying my medication from the only place I can afford it, and I'm taking it the way my doctor told me to take it back when I could still afford a doctor. That's what's happening."

She got back in the Civic. The engine turned over on the second try — a hesitation, then the catch, the sound of a car that started every morning and might stop one day without warning. Carmen watched her pull out of the lot. Brake lights at the exit, a left signal, and the silver car merged into the afternoon traffic on Route 27 and was gone.

Carmen went in through the front doors with four minutes left before shift. The silk flowers Cliff had put out for the survey were still on the reception desk. Her badge hit her scrub pocket when she clipped it on.

In the medication room, the day nurse was waiting with the controlled-substance log open beside a fresh pharmacy tote. Burt Kovacs's refill sheet was clipped to the top: generic carbidopa-levodopa substituted again. In the return bin, a discontinued trazodone card held twenty-six tablets, sealed, expiration next spring.

Last week those would have been two separate chores. Burt's nausea. A return bin. Something to initial, something to hand off, something to forget.

She counted with the day nurse, wrote the official number where the form required it, left the second count unspoken, and went out to start med pass.
state_deltas_required: Burt's fall threatens exposure and forces the ring to confront the fact that its members are aging and fragile
Carmen conceals physical evidence and calls herself part of 'us' for the first time; Burt's decline shows biology is the ring's real adversary
